descriptive_long.solution:
B-1. The change of variables \( midvar \rightarrow 1-midvar \) yields
\[
\begin{aligned}
\int_{0}^{1} \int_{0}^{1} \cdots & \int_{0}^{1} \cos ^{2}\left\{\frac{\pi}{2 dimsize}\left(firstvar+secondv+\cdots+nthvar\right)\right\} d firstvar d secondv \cdots d nthvar \\
& =\int_{0}^{1} \int_{0}^{1} \cdots \int_{0}^{1} \sin ^{2}\left\{\frac{\pi}{2 dimsize}\left(firstvar+secondv+\cdots+nthvar\right)\right\} d firstvar d secondv \cdots d nthvar
\end{aligned}
\]

Each of these expressions, being equal to half their sum, must equal \( \frac{1}{2} \). The limit is also \( \frac{1}{2} \).

descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
B-1. The change of variables \( harmonica \rightarrow 1-harmonica \) yields
\[
\begin{aligned}
\int_{0}^{1} \int_{0}^{1} \cdots & \int_{0}^{1} \cos ^{2}\left\{\frac{\pi}{2 blueparrot}\left(lemonseed+candlewick+\cdots+tumbleweed\right)\right\} d lemonseed d candlewick \cdots d tumbleweed \\
& =\int_{0}^{1} \int_{0}^{1} \cdots \int_{0}^{1} \sin ^{2}\left\{\frac{\pi}{2 blueparrot}\left(lemonseed+candlewick+\cdots+tumbleweed\right)\right\} d lemonseed d candlewick \cdots d tumbleweed
\end{aligned}
\]

Each of these expressions, being equal to half their sum, must equal \( \frac{1}{2} \). The limit is also \( \frac{1}{2} \).

descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
B-1. The change of variables \( constantgeneric \rightarrow 1-constantgeneric \) yields
\[
\begin{aligned}
\int_{0}^{1} \int_{0}^{1} \cdots & \int_{0}^{1} \cos ^{2}\left\{\frac{\pi}{2 unknownvalue}\left(constantone+constanttwo+\cdots+constantfinal\right)\right\} d constantone d constanttwo \cdots d constantfinal \\
& =\int_{0}^{1} \int_{0}^{1} \cdots \int_{0}^{1} \sin ^{2}\left\{\frac{\pi}{2 unknownvalue}\left(constantone+constanttwo+\cdots+constantfinal\right)\right\} d constantone d constanttwo \cdots d constantfinal
\end{aligned}
\]

Each of these expressions, being equal to half their sum, must equal \( \frac{1}{2} \). The limit is also \( \frac{1}{2} \).

garbled_string.solution:
B-1. The change of variables \( mvnslqer \rightarrow 1-mvnslqer \) yields
\[
\begin{aligned}
\int_{0}^{1} \int_{0}^{1} \cdots & \int_{0}^{1} \cos ^{2}\left\{\frac{\pi}{2 fkjdlswe}\left(qzxwvtnp+hjgrksla+\cdots+bwpcdfoh\right)\right\} d qzxwvtnp d hjgrksla \cdots d bwpcdfoh \\
& =\int_{0}^{1} \int_{0}^{1} \cdots \int_{0}^{1} \sin ^{2}\left\{\frac{\pi}{2 fkjdlswe}\left(qzxwvtnp+hjgrksla+\cdots+bwpcdfoh\right)\right\} d qzxwvtnp d hjgrksla \cdots d bwpcdfoh
\end{aligned}
\]

Each of these expressions, being equal to half their sum, must equal \( \frac{1}{2} \). The limit is also \( \frac{1}{2} \).

kernel_variant.question:
Let n\ge 1 be an integer.  Evaluate
\[
I_n\;=\;\int_{0}^{1}\!\int_{0}^{1}\!\cdots\!\int_{0}^{1} \sin^{2}\Bigl(\frac{\pi}{2n}(x_1+x_2+\cdots+x_n)\Bigr)\,dx_1\,dx_2\cdots dx_n.
\]
Show that the value of I_n is the same for every n and determine this common value.
kernel_variant.solution:
Set up the n-fold integral

I_n = \int _{[0,1]^n} sin^2\bigl(\tfrac{\pi }{2n}(x_1 + \cdots  + x_n)\bigr) d x_1\ldots d x_n.

1. Symmetry substitution. For each coordinate make the change of variables x_k \to  1-x_k.  The Jacobian is 1, and [0,1]^n is mapped onto itself, so

  I_n = \int _{[0,1]^n} sin^2\bigl(\tfrac{\pi }{2n}((1-x_1)+\ldots +(1-x_n))\bigr) d x
      = \int _{[0,1]^n} sin^2\bigl(\tfrac{\pi }{2n}(n-(x_1+\ldots +x_n))\bigr) d x.

Since sin(\pi /2 - \theta ) = cos \theta , the integrand becomes

  cos^2\bigl(\tfrac{\pi }{2n}(x_1+\ldots +x_n)\bigr).

Hence

  I_n = \int _{[0,1]^n} cos^2\bigl(\tfrac{\pi }{2n}(x_1+\ldots +x_n)\bigr) d x.

2. Complementary integrals. We now have both

  I_n = \int  sin^2(\ldots ) d x
  and
  I_n = \int  cos^2(\ldots ) d x.

Adding gives

  2I_n = \int (sin^2 + cos^2) d x = \int 1 d x = 1,

since the volume of [0,1]^n is 1.

3. Therefore

  I_n = 1/2.

Conclusion: For every positive integer n, I_n = 1/2, independent of n.

core_steps: Exploit symmetry of the cube via the substitution x_k → 1 − x_k.
Under this change, cos²(θ) turns into sin²(θ) with the same θ.
Since cos²θ + sin²θ = 1, the two equal integrals each equal 1/2.
Integral value is independent of n, so the stated limit is 1/2.

slot1.description: Which squared trig function is written in the statement; the proof works identically if the other one is used, because the change of variables swaps them.

slot2.description: Whether the problem asks for the value for a fixed n, for all n, or for the limit as n → ∞; the integral is independent of n so this wording change has no effect on the argument.
